title: Oat milk body lotion
subtitle: A water-in-oil lotion with oat milk in the water phase, emulsified with BTMS-free emulsifying wax
excerpt: This lotion has a water phase built on oat milk, which brings beta-glucan and soothing properties to a standard body lotion base. Because it contains water, it requires a broad-spectrum preservative. The emulsifying wax binds the water and oil phases together into a stable, pourable lotion.
sourceNotes: Water-in-oil lotion formulation from Point of Interest blog and Natural Cosmetics Formulation Handbook. Oat milk beta-glucan properties from peer-reviewed dermatology literature.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Keep all equipment scrupulously clean to avoid contamination.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use once cooled and homogeneous. Label with date made.
recipe.temperatureNote: Heat both water phase and oil phase separately to 70 degrees Celsius before combining.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months with a broad-spectrum preservative. Water-containing products support microbial growth; do not extend beyond the preservative's tested efficacy. Discard if the lotion separates (other than brief shaking), smells off, or shows any mould.

recipeTools.notes: One for water phase, one for oil phase

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of emulsifiers that stabilises water-in-oil or oil-in-water lotions by preventing phase separation. Used at 5 to 8% of the total formula weight in most body lotions.

A chemical or blend of chemicals that inhibits bacterial, yeast, and mould growth in water-containing cosmetics. Any product containing water requires a broad-spectrum preservative at the manufacturer's recommended usage rate.
The water-soluble ingredients in a lotion formula, heated separately before combining with the oil phase. In this recipe the water phase includes oat milk, distilled water, and glycerine.

The oil-soluble ingredients in a lotion formula, including oils, butters, and emulsifying wax. Both phases must be at the same temperature (around 70 degrees Celsius) when combined to ensure stable emulsification.
A polysaccharide naturally present in oats with well-documented skin-soothing and barrier-supporting properties. It remains partially active in a lotion formula.

to bind both phases together. Because this formula contains water, a
is non-negotiable. Shelf life with preservative is 3 months. Shelf life without preservative is not meaningful as bacterial contamination can occur within days.

items.prepNote: Cool-down phase; 1% of formula; use at manufacturer's recommended rate

content.text: Weigh the water phase ingredients (oat milk and distilled water) into one heat-resistant bowl and heat to 70 degrees Celsius. In a separate bowl, weigh the oil phase ingredients (sweet almond oil, shea butter, emulsifying wax) and heat to 70 degrees Celsius until the emulsifying wax has fully melted.

Pour the water phase slowly into the oil phase while mixing continuously with a stick blender on low. The mixture will immediately begin to emulsify and turn white and creamy. Continue blending for 3 to 5 minutes until the lotion is homogeneous.
Allow to cool to below 40 degrees Celsius. Add the glycerine, preservative, and lavender essential oil and stir to incorporate. Pour into a clean jar. Label with the date made.

Store at room temperature, away from direct sunlight. Shelf life is 3 months with a broad-spectrum preservative. Discard if the lotion separates and does not re-combine with shaking, develops a sour or off smell, or shows any visible contamination. Use a pump dispenser or clean spatula to prevent introducing bacteria from fingers into the jar.
